Egypt.title: Pyramids
Egypt.description: The Pyramids and other tombs are massive marvels of engineering, built surprisingly early in Egyptian history by warring rulers who liked to flex in an attempt at legitimacy.

Egypt.title: Hatshepsut
Egypt.description: A rare female Pharaoh, Hatshepsut focuses on trade and building projects for a peaceful and prosperous reign. She was erased and slandered after her death.

Egypt.title: Akhenaten and the Aten
Egypt.description: Akhenaten forces massive cultural, political and religious changes, privileging the sun-disk (Aten) to the destruction of other gods. His cult is widely hated and dies alongside him.

Egypt.title: Cleopatra
Egypt.description: Highly intelligent, educated and magnetic, Cleopatra allied with Caesar to defeat her brother-husband and become sole Pharoah. She ruled actively and benevolently before being killed in a Roman succession crisis.

Assyria.title: Tablets of Kanesh
Assyria.description: Our understanding of Ancient Anatolia comes from a collection of clay tablets found at Kanesh, the letters and records of a town of traders who built on family ties to create complex economic systems of debt and ownership.

Assyria.title: Esarhaddon
Assyria.description: Paranoid, Esarhaddon would perform 'Substitute King' 7 times in his reign to go farm. Still, he managed to conquer Egypt and rebuild Babylon before leaving a strong realm behind in a smooth transition.

Carthage.title: Queen Dido
Carthage.description: Fleeing her murderous brother, the King of Tyre, Dido was crowned in Carthage after cunningly creating the country out of cowhide. She cameos in the Aeneid as a temporary love interest.

England.title: Robin Hood
England.description: Richard Lionheart abandons duty to Crown for duty to Crusade, so bro John sneakily takes the reigns. Robin Hood rebels in accordance with popular sentiment, and John's eventual Kingship becomes pyrrhic when it's followed with the Magna Carta.

England.title: War of the Roses
England.description: Go watch House of the Dragon for a semi-accurate recreation.

England.title: South Sea Bubble
England.description: Hype for riches builds as British politicians are bribed into the South Sea Company. Govt debt gets handed over alongside the everyman's life savings, until it all pops.

England.title: Steam Powered Loom
England.description: The Industrial Revolution kicks off properly with the invention of an automatic loom, accompanied by very controversial job loss. The Luddites protest loudly as the Marxist era of capitalism properly kicks off.

New Zealand.title: Early sea voyages
New Zealand.description: Early migration of Lapita culture from Taiwan island-hopped through the Philippines, Papua New Guinea, Melanesia. Voyages went 30-300 km and took 1-4 days, with continuous evidence of land proximity.

New Zealand.title: Early ocean voyages
New Zealand.description: Migrating eastwards meant canoeing 500-2000 km, from 4 days to 3 weeks. Polynesians created sophisticated navigation: star lines, swell refraction patterns, migration patterns, string web mapping. Occupation of Vanuatu, Fiji, Tonga, Samoa, etc.

New Zealand.title: New Zealand's 'discovered'
New Zealand.description: English Captain Cook and French Captain de Surville sail to NZ at the same time. de Surville dies soon, Cook survives; circumnavigates and charts “New Zealand” which labels him 'founder'.

New Zealand.title: Great ocean voyages
New Zealand.description: 400 years of extraordinary Polynesian ocean exploration, crossing 1500-8000km per voyage, taking 2 months between landfall. Occupied entire Pacific; probably reached South America given Kumara distribution.

New Zealand.title: Kupe
New Zealand.description: Sailing from Hawaiki in his waka Matahorua, Kupe discovered Aotearoa. First landed at Hokianga before circumnavigating Te Ika-a-Māui (North) and northwest of Te Waipounamu (South). Returned to Hawaiki.

New Zealand.title: The Great Fleet
New Zealand.description: Many of Kupe's grandkids's gen migrated to New Zealand, with up to 40 legendary vessels with family/tribal groups fleeing resource competition and conflict at home. Each established control over various territories.

New Zealand.title: Abel Tasman
New Zealand.description: Tasman “discovers” “Nova Zeelandia”, 4 Dec 1642, sailing from Jakarta seeking new trade opportunities for the Dutch East India Company. Anchored near Golden Bay but didn't land as four sailors were killed in fight with Māori.

New Zealand.title: Increasing European contact
New Zealand.description: In 1787, Australia becomes a British penal colony. NZ <> Europe trade increases: timber, flax, food. More over time: missionaries, whale/seal oil/furs. further grew shipping and coastal trade.

New Zealand.title: Musket Wars
New Zealand.description: European trade introduces guns to the already-warlike Maori, escalating existing conflicts between tribes into an arms race. Longer campaigns (sustained by potatoes) killed 20% of Maori.

New Zealand.title: Independence
New Zealand.description: 1835: 52 (mostly northern) tribes signed He Whakaputanga o te Rangatiratanga o Nu Tireni ( Declaration of Independence of the United Tribes of NZ). 1836: British Parliament acknowledges.

New Zealand.title: Darwin visits
New Zealand.description: Charles Darwin visits on board the Beagle in 1835. By then, 3-5 sailing vessels anchored at Kororāreka (Russell, Bay of Islands) were a common sight

New Zealand.title: The New Zealand Company
New Zealand.description: The New Zealand Company bought land for trinkets to on-sell for huge margins. EG Wakefield snaked ahead of Royal law to colonize, with a plan he'd made while in prison for kidnapping and marrying a 15-year-old heiress.

New Zealand.title: Treaty of Waitangi
New Zealand.description: 6 Feb 1840: Te Tiriti o Waitangi established the relationship between the Crown and Māori. 540 Maori signed the Te Reo treaty. 24 Chiefs signed an English version. There were… differences.

New Zealand.title: Wairau, Wellington, Wanganui
New Zealand.description: June 1843. Dodgy land deals by NZCo cause Wairau Incident: Te Rauparaha vs Arthur Wakefield. AW + 21 settlers killed, 4 Māori dead. Further fights in Wellington, Wanganui. Gov Fitzroy's inquiry found for the Māori, raising colonists' anger.

New Zealand.title: Flagstaff War
New Zealand.description: Hōne Heke was first to sign Te Tiriti o Waitangi & donated national Flagstaff. But later Crown usurpation of Māori authority got so bad he chopped the Flagstaff down 4x. Led to battles and fortification in Auckland.

New Zealand.title: Taranaki and Waikato
New Zealand.description: Settler pop overtakes Māori. Governance now local instead of Crown Office. Māori unite in Kingi movement in protest, block land sales. Dodgy deal pretext for 19k trained settler troops to battle 3k Māori, then alienate Māori from confiscated NI land.

New Zealand.title: New Zealand (a.k.a. Land) Wars
New Zealand.description: New Zealand (Land) Wars: Brits ignore their own treaty due to racism and greed. Two decades later Māori fight to assert their treaty rights against the Crown, the New Zealand Company, etc. Turns into British war of conquest.

New Zealand.title: Parihaka
New Zealand.description: Te Whiti and Tohu leads village Parihaka in nonviolent resistance. Attracts Māori from all NZ, global renown, influences Gandhi. Invaded on 5 Nov 1881. The government troops applied scorched earth tactics for three years to stifle the movement.

Netherlands.title: Tulip Bubble
Netherlands.description: Quick to bloom, quicker to doom. Exotic tulips signalled status, causing one of the earliest speculative bubbles as all Amsterdam spent big on bulb bonds.

Byzantium.title: Emperor Justinian
Byzantium.description: Weds 'actress' Theodora. Rebel Nika riots, plague, war. Persia peace and reconquered Rome(+Egypt) imperiled by shaky trust in Belisarius. Lasting legislative legacy: efficient taxes and Corpus Juris Civilis. Hagia Sophia, Monophysite, feminism.

Rome.title: Punic War 2
Rome.description: Hannibal Jr leads Carthage to victory against Rome in reprise war with surprise elephants. Despite with Senate resistance, Scipio innovates back. “Cowardly” tactics gets Rome the final victory. Much genius new military tactics (eg: envelopment) on both sides.

Rome.title: Social War
Rome.description: City-state Rome has hegemony over Italy. Non-citizens are legally lesser and upset about it. Italian cities seeking integration or independence form a coalition. The Social War results in a unified polity across Italy with wider citizenship.

Rome.title: Julius Caesar
Rome.description: Civil war, land reform, massive conquests

Rome.title: Pompeii
Rome.description: Mt Vesuvius erupts in 79AD, burying Pompeii in mud and ash. Sad as it was for the populace at the time, later historians are grateful for this snapshot of Roman life.

Rome.title: Crisis of the 3rd Century
Rome.description: Rome's ongoing decline tips to outright catastrophe. Fatal inflation as denarii lose silver. Coups, killings, 30+ emperors in 50 years: fragmented power. Diocletian's East/West Tetrarchy solution admits empire's centralization has disintegrated.

Achaemenid.title: Cyrus the Great
Achaemenid.description: Conquers the Golden Crescent into a Golden Age, with religious tolerance and local self-governance. He declares human rights on the Cyrus Cylinder and becomes Messiah by freeing the Jews.

Achaemenid.title: Darius I
Achaemenid.description: Cyrus's son Bardiya was slain by his brother in secret, allowing a Magian impersonator to claim the throne. Thus t'was righteous vengeance when guardsman Darius I slew the imposter. Alternatively, a skeptic might call him a king-slaying ursurper.

Ethiopia.title: Queen of Sheba
Ethiopia.description: Folklore holds that King Solomon seduced the African Queen of Sheba, who returned to Ethiopia for their son Menelik to found the Solomonic dynasty, making the country a rare Christian holdout in Africa.

Ethiopia.title: Gudit
Ethiopia.description: A Warrior Queen claims Ethiopia and rules for 40yrs, her descendants ruling after. She's a muddled figure in myth, a church-burning possibly-Jewish possibly-pagan conqueror. Her dynasty is considered a Dark Age external to Ethiopia's historical flow.

Ethiopia.title: Kebra Nagast
Ethiopia.description: A religious bloodline epic serving to establish the Ethiopian monarchs as direct descendants of Solomon. It is skillfully woven with the historical record, but likely largely invented in search of divine legitimacy after a Crown-by-Conquest.

Ethiopia.title: Mentewab
Ethiopia.description: Co-regnant with her son, and holds power peacefully for decades, until her Oromo DIL contests the regency for her grandson. Their powerful families fight each other to such weakness that outsider Mikael Sehul can snake control, causing an interregnum.
